bd-1.title: Use model packs for summarization (cost waste fix)
bd-1.description: summarization_agent.py uses get_global_model_name() instead of get_model_for_role('summarizer'). This causes expensive main model (e.g. claude-opus) to be used for summarization instead of cheap model (gemini-2.5-flash). The infrastructure exists but isn't being used.

bd-2.title: Cost estimator uses wrong model for sub-agents
bd-2.description: Cost estimator defaults to gpt-4o for invoke_agent calls instead of looking up actual sub-agent model. Makes /cost inaccurate.

bd-3.title: Prevent retry stacking (3 streaming × 5 HTTP = 15 retries)
bd-3.description: Multiple retry layers can stack causing up to 15 retries per request. Add shared retry budget awareness.

bd-4.title: Replace heuristic token counting with provider-accurate counting
bd-4.description: Current 4.0-4.5 chars/token heuristic has 10-30% error. Affects compaction decisions, read-file gating, cost estimation. Need provider-specific tokenizers.
comments:
Part 1 complete: Created token_counting.py infrastructure module.

New module provides provider-aware token counting:
- detect_provider(): Identifies model providers (openai, anthropic, google, unknown)
- count_tokens_openai(): Tiktoken-accurate counting for OpenAI models  
- count_tokens_anthropic(): Uses tiktoken cl100k_base approximation for Anthropic
- count_tokens(): Provider-aware routing with graceful fallback to heuristic
- count_messages_tokens(): Message list counting with role overhead (~4 tokens/msg)

Verified:
- All provider detection cases pass (gpt*, o1, o3, claude*, gemini*)
- Tiktoken integration working (encoding cache with 8-slot LRU)
- Heuristic fallback produces accurate results (~4 chars/token)
- Empty text edge case handled (returns 0)
- Code formatted with ruff, linting passes

Part 2 will wire this into token_utils.py and critical decision points (context compaction, read-file safety gates, cost estimation).

bd-5.title: Unify message_limit defaults (10x sub-agent budget fix)
bd-5.description: Sub-agents use get_message_limit() with default=1000 while main agent uses cfg.message_limit with default=100. This allows sub-agents 10x more request budget than intended. Fix: Change get_message_limit default to 100 and make agent_tools.py use cfg.message_limit.
comments: Fixed by unifying message_limit defaults. Changed get_message_limit() default from 1000 to 100, and updated agent_tools.py to use get_puppy_config().message_limit instead of get_message_limit(). Sub-agents now have same request limit as main agent.

bd-6.title: Create centralized resolve_max_output_tokens() function
bd-6.description: Output caps are inconsistent: auto-calc (15% context), Antigravity (64k-128k), Codex (none). Create single function to resolve caps consistently.

bd-7.title: turbo_ops token estimator alignment with core
bd-7.description: Token accounting audit found that turbo_ops uses a fixed 2.5 chars/token while the rest of the codebase uses 4.0-4.5 chars/token depending on content type. This makes file reads from turbo_ops look ~1.6x-1.8x heavier than they should. Fix: Change estimate_tokens() in turbo_ops/src/models.rs from 2.5 to 4.0 chars/token.

bd-8.title: Fix ctx.close() bug - callbacks.py calls ctx.close() but RunContext only has end()

bd-9.title: Add config cache invalidation - typed config stays stale after set_value()
comments: Claimed: adding typed config cache invalidation via lazy import of reset_puppy_config_for_tests() in set_config_value() and reset_value()

bd-10.title: Integrate provider-reported usage into TokenLedger from all model clients

bd-11.title: Unify protected_token_count defaults - 50000 in config.py vs 4000 in config_package

bd-12.title: Fix compaction strategy default - docstring says summarization but code returns truncation

bd-13.title: Fix Rust/Python system prompt double-counting for Claude Code and Antigravity models

bd-14.title: Make TokenLedger thread-safe with proper locking

bd-15.title: Create unified config interface for compaction settings

bd-16.title: Track retry costs separately in TokenLedger

bd-17.title: Fix tool-schema LRU cache unhashable key causing silent overhead undercounting

bd-18.title: Add pre-send context assertion to prevent context overflow

bd-19.title: [zig] Phase 4: Production cutover and Rust deprecation
bd-19.description:
Switch from Rust to Zig acceleration in production:

Tasks:
- [ ] Add ZIG_ACCELERATION feature flag
- [ ] Update _core_bridge.py to prefer Zig over Rust
- [ ] A/B test Zig vs Rust performance
- [ ] Update CI to build Zig wheels
- [ ] Remove Rust crates (if Zig passes all tests)

Acceptance criteria:
- Zig modules pass all integration tests
- Performance is >= Rust
- Clean deprecation path documented
- CI produces Zig wheels for all platforms

Depends on: Phase 2, Phase 3
comments:
Updated for hybrid cutover approach:

Hybrid Architecture:
- Rust (PyO3): puppy_core, turbo_parse (performance critical)
- Zig (cffi): turbo_ops (file I/O, less FFI sensitive)

This replaces the full Rust deprecation plan. The hybrid approach leverages:
- Rust's 80x lower FFI overhead for hot paths (message processing, parsing)
- Zig's simpler build/cross-compilation for file operations (less FFI sensitive)

Completed:
- [x] Implement unified acceleration bridge (code_puppy/acceleration/)

Tasks to complete:
- [ ] Add feature flags for hybrid backend selection
- [ ] Integrate zig_bridge.list_files and zig_bridge.grep into tools/
- [ ] Update CI to build both Rust and Zig extensions
- [ ] Performance benchmark: hybrid vs pure Rust vs pure Python
- [ ] Document hybrid architecture in docs/acceleration.md
Starting Phase 4: Production cutover with hybrid architecture
Phase 4 complete: Hybrid Zig/Rust production cutover finished. All features merged.

bd-22.title: [zig] Phase 2.5: MessagePack binary protocol for performance
bd-22.description:
Replace JSON serialization with MessagePack to close performance gap with Rust.

Tasks:

comments: Starting MessagePack implementation - aiming for 5-10x speedup over current JSON path and closing the gap to <10x vs Rust native (currently at 160x). Will prioritize a minimal serde.zig integration first.
Binary protocol implemented. FFI overhead (~2ms) is the bottleneck, not serialization. PyO3 has fundamentally lower overhead than cffi/ctypes.

bd-23.description:

bd-24.title: Agent Flow V2 Phase 2: CLI Live Visualization
bd-24.description:
Implement live CLI visualization for Agent Trace V2 data. All trace information should be displayed directly in the terminal during agent execution - no separate website, no slash commands needed to view.

## Goals
Display trace data automatically in CLI as agents run:
- Show model calls as separate nodes from agent runs (tree view)
- Display token counts with accounting state indicators (estimated vs exact)
- Show timeline/waterfall view for latency truth
- Context source breakdown (system/history/tool results)
- Real-time updates during streaming

## CLI Display Requirements
1. **Auto-display during execution** - No /slash command needed to see traces
2. **Inline with agent output** - Integrated into normal CLI flow
3. **Compact but informative** - Show key metrics without overwhelming
4. **Accounting state indicators** - Visual distinction for estimated vs exact tokens
5. **Reconciliation feedback** - Show when estimates are corrected

## Suggested CLI Layout
```
┌─ code-puppy (agent) ─────────────────────────────────┐
│ ├─ claude-sonnet-4-20250514 (model) ⏳ streaming...           │
│ │   └─ tokens: ~142 out (estimated)                 │
│ └─ cp_read_file (tool) ✓ 45ms                       │
│       └─ result: 892 tokens → model input           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Tasks
1. Create CLI renderer for TraceState (Rich panels/trees)
2. Hook into stream_event for live updates
3. Add accounting state visual indicators (~estimated, ✓exact, ⟳reconciled)
4. Show token flow: model output → tool args → tool result → model input
5. Display reconciliation when exact usage arrives
6. Add latency/duration display for spans
7. Integrate with existing terminal output (don't break flow)

## Key Files
- code_puppy/plugins/agent_trace/cli_renderer.py (new)
- code_puppy/plugins/agent_trace/register_callbacks.py (extend)

## Success Criteria
- Trace visualization appears automatically during agent runs
- Model calls visually distinct from agent runs  
- Token accounting state is clear at a glance
- No manual commands needed to see trace data
- Works in standard terminal (no special requirements)
comments: Claiming this issue - starting implementation of Phase 2 CLI renderer
Phase 2 complete: CLI live visualization with tree view, accounting state indicators, automatic display during execution. Commit: b7251484

bd-38.title: P0: run_id vs worker_pid bug - Run.Manager passes wrong ID to PythonWorker
bd-38.description:
CRITICAL BUG: Run.Manager.start_run/3 passes worker_pid to PythonWorker.Port.start_run/2 which expects run_id.

**Files:**
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/run/manager.ex:55-71
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/python_worker/port.ex:82-85

**Impact:** Run tracking completely broken - Python worker receives wrong identifier causing mismatched state updates and orphaned runs.

**Fix:** Change Run.Manager to pass run_id (UUID) instead of worker_pid to PythonWorker.Port.start_run/2.

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-39.title: P0: RequestTracker race condition - Fast responses lost before registration
bd-39.description:
CRITICAL RACE CONDITION: Request is sent to Python worker BEFORE registering in RequestTracker. Fast responses arrive before registration completes, causing lost responses and hanging requests.

**Files:**
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/python_worker/port.ex:175-195

**Impact:** Requests can hang indefinitely if Python responds faster than Elixir can register the request in RequestTracker. Under load, this causes cascading failures.

**Fix:** Register request in RequestTracker BEFORE sending to Python. Use a 'pending' state that transitions to 'waiting' after send.

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-40.title: P0: Protocol mismatch - Python newline-JSON vs Elixir Content-Length framing
bd-40.description:
CRITICAL PROTOCOL MISMATCH: Python bridge uses newline-delimited JSON, but Elixir expects Content-Length framing. Messages are truncated or concatenated incorrectly.

**Files:**
- code_puppy/plugins/elixir_bridge/register_callbacks.py:146-150,170-172
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/protocol.ex

**Impact:** Protocol corruption, message parsing failures, truncated or concatenated frames causing undefined behavior.

**Fix:** Align both sides to use the SAME framing:
- Option A: Add Content-Length framing to Python side
- Option B: Use newline-delimited JSON on Elixir side
- Document the chosen protocol explicitly

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-41.title: P0: sys.pid bug - Python bridge emits sys.pid which doesn't exist
bd-41.description:
CRITICAL BUG: Python bridge emits 'sys.pid' field that doesn't exist in Python's sys module. Should be 'os.getpid()'.

**Files:**
- code_puppy/plugins/elixir_bridge/register_callbacks.py:70-73

**Impact:** Elixir side receives invalid/None worker PID causing connection tracking failures and inability to correlate Python processes with Elixir runs.

**Fix:** Change sys.pid to os.getpid() in the Python bridge registration message.

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-42.title: P0: Unsafe String.to_atom/1 - External strings converted to atoms
bd-42.description:
CRITICAL SECURITY BUG: External strings (from Python, MCP clients) are converted to atoms using String.to_atom/1, causing atom table exhaustion (VM crash vulnerability).

**Files:**
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/python_worker/port.ex:322
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/run/state.ex:370

**Impact:** 
- Denial of service via atom table exhaustion
- VM crash after ~1M unique strings
- No input validation on external data

**Fix:** 
- Use String.to_existing_atom/1 with fallback
- Or use binary keys for external data
- Add input validation layer

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-43.title: P0: stderr merged into protocol stream - Corrupts frame parser
bd-43.description:
CRITICAL BUG: Python worker uses :stderr_to_stdout option which merges stderr into stdout, corrupting the protocol stream and causing frame parsing failures.

**Files:**
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/python_worker/port.ex:150-156

**Impact:** Any Python warning/error (including deprecation warnings, stack traces) corrupts the protocol stream, causing cascading parse failures and system instability.

**Fix:** 
- Remove :stderr_to_stdout from Port.open
- Capture stderr separately via :stderr or logging
- Add protocol stream sanitization

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-44.title: P0: Run.Supervisor bypassed - App starts raw DynamicSupervisor
bd-44.description:
CRITICAL SUPERVISION BUG: Application starts a raw DynamicSupervisor instead of the custom CodePuppyControl.Run.Supervisor module, breaking restart strategies and run isolation.

**Files:**
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/application.ex:24-39

**Impact:** 
- Run isolation broken - runs can interfere with each other
- Restart strategies not applied
- No proper supervision tree for run processes
- Potential cascade failures

**Fix:** Change Application.start to use CodePuppyControl.Run.Supervisor instead of raw DynamicSupervisor with :simple_one_for_one strategy.

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-45.title: P0: MCP framing mismatch - Double framing with {:packet,4} + Content-Length
bd-45.description:
CRITICAL BUG: MCP server uses BEAM {:packet, 4} framing PLUS manual Content-Length parsing, causing double-framing issues and ID type mismatch (integer vs string).

**Files:**
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/mcp/server.ex:323-350
- zig_src/process_runner/protocol.zig

**Impact:** 
- Message truncation/concatenation
- ID type mismatches between Elixir (integer) and Zig (string)
- Protocol parsing failures on MCP stdio bridge

**Fix:** 
- Choose ONE framing method (prefer manual Content-Length for MCP compliance)
- Remove {:packet, 4} from socket options
- Normalize ID types to strings throughout

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-46.title: P1: Remove Zig duplication - Remove puppy_core, turbo_ops, turbo_parse Zig crates
bd-46.description:
HIGH SEVERITY: Remove redundant Zig crates that duplicate Rust functionality. Keep only process_runner temporarily for process management.

**Scope:**
- Remove zig_src/puppy_core (duplicate of code_puppy_core Rust)
- Remove zig_src/turbo_ops (duplicate of turbo_ops Rust)
- Remove zig_src/turbo_parse (duplicate of turbo_parse Rust)
- Keep zig_src/process_runner temporarily (unique functionality)

**Rationale:**
- No benefit to maintaining two implementations
- Rust versions are primary and better tested
- Focus resources on single implementation

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] puppy_core removed from build.zig
- [ ] turbo_ops removed from build.zig
- [ ] turbo_parse removed from build.zig
- [ ] process_runner kept with proper tests
- [ ] Documentation updated

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-47.title: P1: Unbounded history lists - request_history and events grow without limit
bd-47.description:
HIGH SEVERITY: Run.State stores request_history and events as unbounded lists, causing unbounded memory growth over long-running sessions.

**Files:**
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/run/state.ex:203-204

**Impact:** 
- Unbounded memory growth
- OOM crashes on long sessions
- No eviction policy
- Performance degradation over time

**Fix:** 
- Add configurable history limits (e.g., max 1000 entries)
- Implement ring buffer or circular eviction
- Add :history_limit config option
- Log warning when history is truncated

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-48.title: P1: Event tuple mismatch - Run.State vs EventBus send different formats
bd-48.description:
HIGH SEVERITY: Run.State expects {:run_event, event} tuple but EventBus sends {:event, event}, causing event processing failures.

**Files:**
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/run/state.ex
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/event_bus.ex

**Impact:** 
- Events not processed correctly
- Run state updates lost
- Event-driven features broken
- Silent failures in event handling

**Fix:** Standardize on ONE event tuple format:
- Option A: Change Run.State to expect {:event, event}
- Option B: Change EventBus to send {:run_event, event}
- Document the chosen format

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-49.title: P2: WebSocket unauthenticated - UserSocket allows any client connection
bd-49.description:
MEDIUM SEVERITY: UserSocket does not authenticate clients, allowing any WebSocket connection to subscribe to run events and potentially access sensitive data.

**Files:**
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control_web/channels/user_socket.ex

**Impact:** 
- Unauthorized access to run events
- Information disclosure
- No connection authentication/authorization

**Fix:** 
- Add token-based authentication on connect
- Verify session/run ownership before allowing channel join
- Implement authorization checks for run: events

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-50.title: P2: Missing telemetry - No :telemetry instrumentation in Elixir control plane
bd-50.description:
MEDIUM SEVERITY: No :telemetry instrumentation in the Elixir control plane, making production observability and debugging difficult.

**Missing Instrumentation:**
- Run lifecycle events (start, complete, fail)
- Request latency histograms
- Python worker message queue depth
- MCP connection metrics
- WebSocket connection stats

**Impact:** 
- Blind to production issues
- No performance metrics
- Difficult to diagnose bottlenecks
- No alerting capability

**Fix:** 
- Add :telemetry dependency
- Instrument all critical paths
- Define standard metric names
- Document metric semantics

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-51.title: P2: Config sprawl - Multiple config surfaces need consolidation
bd-51.description:
MEDIUM SEVERITY: Configuration is scattered across multiple surfaces with no single source of truth, making deployment and environment management difficult.

**Config Surfaces:**
- Application environment (config/runtime.exs)
- Python config (config.json)
- Zig build options (build.zig)
- Environment variables (inconsistent naming)
- Compile-time vs runtime config confusion

**Impact:** 
- Hard to configure correctly
- Environment-specific bugs
- Deployment complexity
- No validation of config values

**Fix:** 
- Centralize config in one module
- Add config validation at startup
- Document all config options
- Standardize environment variable naming (PUP_ prefix)
- Fail fast on invalid config

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-52.title: P1: MCP.Server blocking receive - Raw receive inside GenServer callbacks
bd-52.description:
HIGH SEVERITY: MCP.Server uses blocking 'receive' inside GenServer.handle_info/2, blocking the GenServer and potentially deadlocking the supervision tree.

**Files:**
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/mcp/server.ex:352-385

**Impact:** 
- MCP server blocks on message processing
- Cannot handle concurrent MCP requests
- GenServer mailbox backup under load
- Potential supervision tree deadlock

**Fix:** 
- Refactor to use :active, :once TCP mode
- Handle packets as they arrive via handle_info
- Or use :gen_tcp.controlling_process + separate process

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-53.title: P1: Run.Manager polling - Uses Process.sleep(100) instead of event-driven
bd-53.description:
HIGH SEVERITY: Run.Manager uses Process.sleep(100) polling loop instead of event-driven architecture (GenStage, PubSub, or message passing).

**Files:**
- elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/run/manager.ex:190-254

**Impact:** 
- 10ms latency overhead minimum
- Wasted CPU cycles on polling
- Scalability ceiling under load
- Non-idiomatic Elixir code

**Fix:** 
- Use PubSub for state change events
- Or GenStage for backpressure
- Or simple message passing GenServer
- Eliminate all polling loops

**Related:** Part of comprehensive Elixir Control Plane audit findings.

bd-54.title: LOW-2: Add rate limiting for API authentication failures
bd-54.description:
**Location:** code_puppy/api/security.py

**Problem:** No rate limiting on failed authentication attempts. An attacker could brute-force API tokens without throttling.

**Fix:** Add rate limiting (e.g., 5 failures per minute per IP):
- Track failed attempts by client IP

bd-58.title: LOW-1: Fix TOCTOU race in OAuth token file permissions
bd-58.description:
**Location:** code_puppy/plugins/claude_code_oauth/utils.py

**Problem:** Token files may be created with insecure permissions briefly before chmod is applied. This is a Time-Of-Check-Time-Of-Use (TOCTOU) race condition.

**Fix:** Use os.open() with mode 0o600 directly instead of open() + chmod():

fd = os.open(token_path, os.O_WRONLY | os.O_CREAT | os.O_TRUNC, 0o600)
with os.fdopen(fd, 'w') as f:
    f.write(token_data)

**Priority:** P3 (Low)
**Category:** Security

bd-120.title: Investigate parallel test isolation failures
bd-120.description: Many tests fail when run with pytest-xdist but pass when run sequentially (-n0). Likely due to shared singleton state (run_limiter, security boundary, etc). Need to audit test fixtures for proper isolation.
comments:
Analysis: 200+ tests fail in parallel but pass sequentially. Root causes appear to be:
1. Shared singletons not being reset between tests (run_limiter, policy_engine, security_boundary)
2. Callback registrations persisting across tests
3. Config file reads during tests (e.g., pack_parallelism.toml)

Fix approaches:
- Add autouse fixtures that reset singletons before each test
- Use pytest marks to force certain tests to run in isolation
- Mock config file reads in affected tests

Priority: Medium - tests pass when run with -n0

Added singleton resets to conftest.py:
- security boundary reset
- run limiter reset
- callbacks reset

This improved some tests but ~291 tests still fail in parallel. The core issues:
- Random test order with pytest-randomly causes different test failures each run
- More singletons exist that need reset (MCP manager, retry manager, etc.)
- Some tests may be inherently non-thread-safe

Tests pass when run individually or in small batches. For CI, recommend using -n0 (sequential) until all isolation issues are fixed.
Claimed by pack-leader-019d9f - Starting systematic singleton audit

New epic filed for remaining parallel test isolation work: bd-125 (Epic: Parallel test isolation — remaining work). Children: bd-126 (diagnose hang), bd-127 (extract helper), bd-128 (wire resets), bd-129 (serial markers), bd-130 (verification).

Progress update: The main hang is fixed (bd-126 through bd-129). Test suite now completes in parallel mode (~55s). However, 263-359 test failures remain. 219 consistent failures identified across runs. Need to fix these actual test failures. See bd-130-verification.md for details.

Investigation complete. The main hang (ThreadPoolExecutor, singleton state) is FIXED. Test suite now completes in parallel mode (~55s). However, 263-359 test failures remain. These appear to be real test bugs, not isolation issues. 219 consistent failures across runs. 184 flaky tests remain. The original premise that 'tests pass sequentially but fail in parallel' is partially true - sequential mode still hangs at 93% due to some hanging tests. The parallel isolation fixes (bd-126 through bd-130) resolved the main hang. Remaining work: fix the actual test failures. See bd-130-verification.md for details.

- Elixir backend (~100 modules in elixir/code_puppy_control/) owns: file ops, parsing, scheduler, message core, text ops, MCP server, indexer, tools, model registry, sessions, routing
- Python layer (~95 files + 49 plugins) still owns: pydantic-ai agent loop, Textual TUI, CLI entry points, plugin callback system, LLM provider SDKs, MCP client, browser automation, command menus, config.py (84KB), adaptive rate limiter, DBOS durable execution, OAuth flows

Goal: Delete the Python tree entirely. End state is a single-binary Elixir release (Burrito) with a native TUI and feature parity with the current Python+Elixir hybrid.

Estimated total effort: ~22-26 weeks across 10 phases.

CRITICAL RISKS:
- R1: pydantic-ai has no Elixir equivalent — match our usage, not the whole lib
- R2: Textual TUI feature gap — prototype in week 1 of Phase 3 before committing
- R3: 49 plugins = long tail — drop Tier-C for v1
- R4: Thinner Elixir provider SDK ecosystem — roll our own on Finch
- R5: config.py is 84KB of accrued semantics — spec before code
- R6: Playwright dependency — keep tiny Python Port OR drop browser tools
- R7: MCP client was pydantic-ai's job — ~1 week focused work
- R8: Session compat — one-shot migrator on first pup-ex launch
- R9: Plugin hot-loading UX trade-off — Elixir compiled plugins, document
- R10: Sheer volume (~400 Python files) — turbo-executor + elixir-dev clones in parallel

comments:
DECISION: Owl (primary) + IO.ANSI (split-panel fallback)

Evaluated all native Elixir TUI options (2026-04-19):

LIBRARY STATUS CHECK:
- Ratatouille 0.5.1: REJECTED — last updated 2020-03-25, effectively dead. ex_termbox NIF backing also dead (same date, same author).
- ex_ncurses 0.3.1: REJECTED — dead since 2018.
- Phoenix LiveView: REJECTED — web framework, not a terminal TUI.
- Owl 0.13.0: SELECTED — last updated 2025-08-29, 1.85M hex downloads, actively maintained, pure Elixir / pure ANSI (no NIFs).
- termbox2_nif 2.0.0: DEFERRED — alive (2025-06-18), keep in reserve if mouse support needed later.

SELECTED STACK:
  Primary:  Owl 0.13.x  — covers 95% of screens (lists, input, spinners, tables, progress bars, streaming output)
  Fallback: IO.ANSI     — for 2-3 screens needing true full-screen canvas (SplitPanel / DiffScreen equivalent)

SCREEN MAPPING (Textual → Owl):
  RichLog (streaming chat)   → Owl.IO + ANSI formatted output
  ListView / ListItem        → Owl.SelectList
  SearchableList widget      → Owl.SelectList with filter input
  Input widget               → Owl.Input.select / Owl.Input.get_line
  OptionList                 → Owl.SelectList
  InfoBar                    → Owl.Box or custom ANSI status line
  SplitPanel (diff viewer)   → IO.ANSI cursor positioning
  Footer (key hints)         → Owl.Table row or plain ANSI line
  Progress / spinner         → Owl.ProgressBar + Owl.Spinner
  Theming                    → Owl.Tag with color atoms

MIGRATION_MAP.md UPDATE NEEDED:
  Line 25: Change 'TUI (Ratatouille)' → 'TUI (Owl + IO.ANSI)'
  mix.exs: Add {:owl, '~> 0.13'} — remove any ratatouille/ex_termbox refs

RISK R2 RESOLUTION: Prototype not required before committing — Owl widget set covers Code Puppy needs without a bake-off. Decision is final.

bd-163.description: Port command_line/ (~30 files, ~300KB) to Elixir. Slash-command router + all built-in commands.
comments:
bd-163 implementation summary:

12 commands ported to Elixir slash-command dispatch:
- Core: /help, /quit, /exit (alias), /clear, /history, /cd (NEW)
- Context: /model, /agent, /sessions, /tui (delegated to existing Loop handlers)
- Session: /compact (stub), /truncate (stub)

30 commands deferred with follow-up tickets:
- bd-244: Menu wizard commands (/add_model, /model_settings, /uc, /mcp install, /autosave, /diff)

- bd-246: /pack, /preset, /workflow small commands (lower priority)
- bd-247: /compact + /truncate full implementation (depends on agent summarization)

Architecture decisions:
1. ETS-backed GenServer registry (named :slash_commands table) with GenServer-serialized writes and concurrent reads
2. Pure Dispatcher module for stateless dispatch logic
3. CommandInfo struct mirrors Python's CommandInfo dataclass with same defaults
4. Commands delegate to existing Loop handlers (defp → def) to minimize churn
5. /exit registered as alias key pointing to /quit's CommandInfo (not a separate command)
6. No silent overwrites on name conflict — returns {:error, {:name_conflict, name}}
7. Registration is explicit via register_builtin_commands/0 called from Application.start/2

New files: 7 lib modules + 4 test files = 11 files
Tests: 54 tests across 4 test files, all passing

SHIPPED: Slash-command dispatch infrastructure + 12 core commands (commits 026fd44e + 27f0fd05).

What shipped:
- 12 core commands ported: /help, /quit, /exit, /clear, /history, /cd, /model, /agent, /sessions, /tui, /compact (stub), /truncate (stub)

bd-165 session storage last mile — GAPS CLOSED:

1. save_session_async/3 — fire-and-forget background save via Task.start/1 (no TaskSupervisor in app tree yet, documented). Mirrors Python's ThreadPoolExecutor pattern. Snapshots history (free in Elixir via immutability), logs warnings on failure, calls mark_autosave_complete on success.

Tracks {last_len, last_hash, last_time, initialized}. 2s debounce window matches Python. Fingerprint via :erlang.term_to_binary + :crypto.hash(:sha256) + Base.encode16[:16].

3. Tests — 12 tests covering all specified scenarios: sync return, disk persistence, failure logging, snapshot isolation, debounce/dedup logic, empty history fingerprint, time injection via :counters.

bd-168.title: 5.1 Build Phoenix server replacing FastAPI (Channels + Plug + PTY)
bd-168.description:
Replace Python FastAPI server with Phoenix. Includes: REST app endpoints, WebSocket handler, PTY manager, security middleware.

CLARIFICATION: This is NOT a line-by-line port of FastAPI-the-framework. It's a native Phoenix implementation providing equivalent behavior using idiomatic Elixir tools:

- REST endpoints → Phoenix.Router + controllers (Plug pipeline)
- WebSocket handler → Phoenix.Channels (topics, presence, broadcast, auto-reconnect)
- PTY manager → Elixir Port / erlexec / NIF
- Security middleware → Plug pipeline (auth, CORS, rate limiting)
- Pub/sub between sessions → Phoenix.PubSub (built-in, no Redis needed)

Use Phoenix-idiomatic patterns throughout. Do NOT replicate FastAPI abstractions in Elixir.

Related: see new sibling issue for Phoenix LiveView web-UI evaluation (potential scope expansion, not part of this task).

bd-181.title: 5.3 Evaluate Phoenix LiveView for optional web UI
bd-181.description:
Decision task: should Code Puppy grow an optional browser-based UI powered by Phoenix LiveView?

Context:
- Today the Python app is TUI-only (Textual) plus a headless FastAPI server.
- Once we're on Phoenix (bd-168), LiveView becomes essentially free.
- LiveView could provide a web-accessible companion UI (remote sessions, team dashboards, browser-based REPL) without a separate SPA.

Options:
A) In-scope for Phase 5 — ship a minimal LiveView companion alongside the Phoenix API.
B) Defer — file follow-up issue for a later phase, ship REST/Channels only.
C) Drop — TUI remains the only frontend; LiveView not pursued.

Acceptance criteria:
- Decision documented with rationale.
- If A: scope + endpoints defined, separate bd issue(s) created for implementation.
- If B: follow-up bd issue filed.
- If C: rationale captured, issue closed as wontfix.

Dependencies: should be decided before or alongside bd-168 to inform Phoenix app structure.

Full doc: docs/decisions/bd-181-liveview-evaluation.md (151 lines, commit 0894ab29).

- TUI-primary user base, zero web-UI demand signal
- bd-168 Phoenix server migration is already Phase 5's largest work

- Option C (drop) rejected as too aggressive

bd-185.title: Plugin porting strategy RFC (Python plugins under Elixir)
bd-185.description:
RFC / decision task. The Python plugins in ~/.code_puppy/plugins/ and code_puppy/plugins/ cannot run directly under Elixir. Decide the strategy before bd-157 (plugin discovery) and bd-158 (port 49 plugins).

Options:
A) Rewrite all plugins in Elixir → cleanest, highest effort, breaks user plugins
B) Python side-car via Port → keep user plugins working, adds runtime complexity, slower
C) Hybrid → rewrite builtin plugins in Elixir, side-car for user plugins
D) Declare plugin API v2 (Elixir-only), provide migration tool + compatibility shim for v1 plugins

Must address:
- User plugin compatibility (is there a breaking change? how loud?)
- Hook API parity (all 20+ hooks in code_puppy/callbacks.py)
- Performance expectations (side-car = IPC overhead per hook call)
- Security model (user plugins are trusted local Python — does that change with Ports?)
- Migration path for plugin authors

Acceptance criteria:
- Decision documented with rationale
- If B/C: IPC protocol spec'd
- If D: migration tool designed + compatibility shim scoped
- bd-157 and bd-158 descriptions updated to reflect chosen strategy

bd-186.title: Dual-home config isolation — Elixir pup-ex never writes to ~/.code_puppy/
bd-186.description:
HARD PREREQUISITE for bd-164, bd-165, bd-166, bd-167, bd-184. Must land before any Elixir code reads or writes config.

## Problem
Last attempt at porting config.py broke the user's ~/.code_puppy/ directory and made Python pup unusable. Root cause: Elixir and Python were sharing a single config home with overlapping read/write surfaces.

## Solution: Dual-Home Isolation

Elixir pup-ex gets its OWN config home: `~/.code_puppy_ex/`
Python pup continues using `~/.code_puppy/` — untouched forever.

## Rules (non-negotiable, CI-enforced)
1. Elixir NEVER writes under ~/.code_puppy/ — no exceptions
2. Elixir reads from ~/.code_puppy/ only via explicit opt-in import commands
3. Python pup is frozen during migration (bug fixes only — see bd-187)
4. OAuth tokens are re-acquired by Elixir independently (no sharing)

## Deliverables

### 1. Config home resolution
- Default: `~/.code_puppy_ex/`
- Overridable via `PUP_EX_HOME` env var
- Created on first run with correct permissions (0700)

### 2. First-run behavior
- If ~/.code_puppy_ex/ doesn't exist: create it empty, show welcome message
- Offer optional import: "Import non-sensitive config from Python pup? [Y/n]"
- If yes: copy puppy.cfg, *_models.json, mcp_registry.json, hooks.json, concurrency.toml, pack_parallelism.toml — with .imported-from-python marker file
- NEVER import: OAuth tokens, sessions, autosaves, databases, command_history
- Fresh install (no ~/.code_puppy/) must work cleanly

### 3. Commands
- `pup-ex import --from ~/.code_puppy` — manual re-sync (copy-only, idempotent, never reverse)
- `pup-ex doctor` — reports isolation status, config paths, detects any bleed-through
- `pup-ex auth login` — runs fresh OAuth flows, stores tokens in ~/.code_puppy_ex/

### 4. Hard runtime guard
- Filesystem path guard: any write attempt to a path canonicalizing under ~/.code_puppy/ raises ConfigIsolationViolation and exits
- Implemented as a Plug-style middleware around all file writes
- Tested via property test that throws random paths at the guard

### 5. CI gates (all must pass)
- test_no_writes_to_python_home: fs-watcher/strace assertion across full test suite
- test_no_reads_of_python_secrets_without_flag: OAuth files only readable with --import-tokens (which we're NOT implementing for now per decision A on OAuth)
- test_import_is_copy_only: before/after diff of ~/.code_puppy/ after import must be empty
- test_fresh_install_works: boot with no ~/.code_puppy/ present
- test_existing_install_untouched: after 10 Elixir runs, find ~/.code_puppy -newer <marker> returns nothing

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 5 CI gates pass
- `pup-ex doctor` shows "✅ Isolated" on a machine with both binaries installed
- Docs updated in MIGRATION_STATUS.md and README.md with dual-home architecture diagram
- bd-164, bd-165, bd-166, bd-167 descriptions updated to reference this isolation requirement

## Design Decisions (user-approved)
- Home path: `~/.code_puppy_ex/` (option 1A)
- OAuth: re-authenticate separately, no sharing with Python tokens (option 2A)
- Python policy: frozen except critical bug fixes (option 3B — see bd-187)

## Blocks
bd-164, bd-165, bd-166, bd-167, bd-184

bd-187.title: Python pup freeze policy during Elixir migration (bug fixes only)
bd-187.description:
Policy: during the Python → Elixir migration, the Python codebase is FROZEN except for critical bug fixes.

## Rules
- NO refactors in code_puppy/ Python tree
- NO schema changes to puppy.cfg or any *.json config files
- NO new features in Python
- YES critical bug fixes (crashes, data loss, security)
- YES adding deprecation warnings that guide users toward pup-ex
- YES docs updates

## Rationale
Python pup is the daily driver. Elixir pup-ex is the work-in-progress replacement. If Python changes, Elixir has to chase a moving target, and the user's working environment is at risk.

## Enforcement
- CI check: PRs touching code_puppy/**/*.py with non-trivial changes require a "bug-fix" or "docs" label
- Reviewer guidance added to CONTRIBUTING.md
- bd issues touching Python must be explicitly approved against this policy

## Acceptance
- CI rule active
- CONTRIBUTING.md section added
- bd template updated with policy checkbox

bd-198.title: fix: graceful handling of dead Elixir transport during autosave rotation and wiggum loop
bd-198.description:
Shutdown/wiggum crash: `rotate_autosave_id()` raises `ElixirTransportError` when the Elixir control-plane subprocess has died, taking down the whole CLI. Root causes: (a) bd-133 left `rotate_autosave_id` / `get_current_autosave_session_name` / `set_current_autosave_from_session_name` / `reset_autosave_id` without the degraded-mode fallback that `get_current_autosave_id` / `get_session_model` received; (b) the wiggum loop in `interactive_loop.py` has no transport-liveness gate and no per-iteration exception wall, so a single transport failure terminates the session. This epic makes autosave rotation crash-proof and wiggum resilient to a dead control plane.

Relates to: bd-133 (pure Elixir routing migration), bd-192 (degraded-mode env var).

bd-206.title: ElixirTransport: BEAM dies between eager startup probe and first runtime_state request
bd-206.description:
## Symptom

Code Puppy crashes during interactive prompt rendering with:

    File "code_puppy/runtime_state.py", line 122, in get_session_model
        result = transport._send_request("runtime_get_session_model", {})
    File "code_puppy/elixir_transport.py", line 379, in _send_request
        raise ElixirTransportError("Transport not started or process died")

The eager probe at app_runner.py:393 ("get_transport()") succeeded — no
"Elixir control-plane failed to start" banner was emitted — so the BEAM
came up healthy. It died sometime between that probe and the first real
request from runtime_state.

## Environment

- Platform: macOS 26.4 arm64
- Python: cpython-3.14.3+freethreaded (3.14t / no-GIL)
- Elixir: 1.19.5 / OTP 28 (erts-16.3.1)
- Working dir: /Users/adam2/projects/code_puppy

## Phase 0 Triage Evidence (already gathered)

1. ✅ "mix code_puppy.stdio_service" boots cleanly standalone, emits
   "_ready" handshake, idles waiting for stdin. Compiler warnings only,
   no fatal errors.
2. ✅ Toolchain healthy: "mix deps.get" no-op, "mix compile" clean.
3. ⚠️ Could not capture BEAM stderr from a real crash session — sub-agent
   triage ran code-puppy without a TTY, hit unrelated prompt_toolkit
   kqueue/fd=0 crash before stressing the transport. Real-terminal capture
   still needed.
4. RuntimeState handler for "runtime_get_session_model" is trivial
   (returns "state.session_model", accepts nil) — no obvious pattern-match
   failure.

## Suspect Window

Recent commits touching transport / singleton:
- c64f3886 — WIP: Elixir control layer progress and Python agent updates
- 183dc4f4 — fix(elixir-transport): thread-safe singleton + degraded mode
              + preflight (bd-192)

bd-192's double-checked-locking singleton in
"elixir_transport_helpers.get_transport()" may interact poorly with no-GIL
Python 3.14t, but this is unverified.

## Hypotheses (ranked)

1. Plugin startup callback (between eager probe and prompt render) sends
   a request that crashes the BEAM; next caller sees a dead pipe.
2. No-GIL race in transport singleton publishes a partially-initialized
   transport whose subprocess pipe gets closed by a concurrent path.
3. SIGPIPE / EPIPE from a stdin-closer somewhere; BEAM exits cleanly but
   Python doesn't notice until next request.
4. Watchdog or signal handler kills BEAM (start_new_session=True is set,
   but something else might be reaching it).

## Reproducer

    cd /Users/adam2/projects/code_puppy
    unset PUP_ALLOW_ELIXIR_DEGRADED
    PUP_LOG_LEVEL=debug code-puppy 2>/tmp/cp-beam-stderr.log
    # crash, then:
    tail -80 /tmp/cp-beam-stderr.log

## Workaround

    export PUP_ALLOW_ELIXIR_DEGRADED=1

routes get_session_model() through a Python-local fallback. Note: not all
runtime_state functions have degraded fallbacks (get_current_autosave_id,
rotate_autosave_id) — those will still crash if invoked.

## Next Steps (acceptance criteria)

- [ ] Capture BEAM stderr from real-terminal reproducer (Action B above)
- [ ] Identify exact root cause from stderr
- [ ] Land targeted fix (likely in elixir_transport.py or
      elixir_transport_helpers.py, or in the plugin path that triggers
      the crash)
- [ ] Add regression test that simulates BEAM dying between requests
- [ ] (Stretch) "_send_request" should distinguish "never started" vs
      "died after start" in its error message — current ambiguity slowed
      triage. One-line fix.
- [ ] (Stretch) "runtime_state.get_current_autosave_id()" and
      "rotate_autosave_id()" should honor "_degraded()" symmetrically
      with the rest of runtime_state — current asymmetric coverage is a
      footgun.
- [ ] (Stretch) Process-death detection should capture last N bytes of
      BEAM stderr on death and include in the error.

## Tangentially related (do NOT bundle, file separately if interested)

User's original request was to fix a FileNotFoundError when agents
invoke_agent("light-coder", ...) — there's no JSON config for light-coder
even though two builtin agent prompts (code-puppy, python-programmer)
reference it. That fix is blocked behind this transport bug. If desired,
file a separate issue: "Ship light-coder agent config (referenced by
builtin prompts but missing from default install)".
comments:
## Headless Reproducer Results (2026-04-19)

**Environment:** macOS, no TTY (stdin-piped via echo), code-puppy 0.0.455

### Action A — Degraded mode boot (PUP_ALLOW_ELIXIR_DEGRADED=1)
- Exit code: 0 (clean)
- Prompt rendered successfully, /exit processed, clean shutdown
- Stderr: only `Warning: Input is not a terminal (fd=0).`
- **Result: No ElixirTransportError.** Degraded mode appears to bypass the transport crash.

### Action B — Non-degraded boot (no PUP_ALLOW_ELIXIR_DEGRADED)
- Exit code: 0 (clean)
- Same clean boot/shutdown as Action A
- Stderr: `Drained 90 stale bytes from stdout during startup` + `Warning: Input is not a terminal (fd=0).`
- BEAM crash signature grep: no matches (no CRASH REPORT, no GenServer EXIT, no EPIPE)
- **Result: ElixirTransportError did NOT reproduce** even without degraded mode.

### Action C — Light-coder smoke test
- The prompt text was echoed but code-puppy exited on stdin EOF before executing it
- **Result: INCONCLUSIVE.** Stdin-piped input cannot drive interactive agent sessions — the agent reads the text as display output, then hits EOF and exits. This cannot verify whether light-coder.json loading works.

### Assessment
- The ElixirTransportError is **not reproducible headless** — it either requires a real TTY (prompt_toolkit init codepath differs), or was intermittent/transient.
- The previously-seen kqueue crash (`OSError: [Errno 22]`) did NOT occur in these runs — the  or EOF was processed before prompt_toolkit tried to poll fd=0.
- Degraded mode boot works as designed (clean exit, no transport errors).
- **Still needs a real TTY test** to definitively confirm whether the original crash persists or was already fixed by recent changes.

PTY smoke test results (2026-04-19):

## PTY Tooling
- Tool used: macOS script(1) + expect(1) — both worked
- script proof: /dev/ttys000 allocated, code-puppy booted to prompt
- expect proof: Full interactive session with prompt_toolkit rendering

## Task 1: Degraded boot under PTY — PASS
- code-puppy booted with PUP_ALLOW_ELIXIR_DEGRADED=1
- Full banner displayed, prompt reached
- No ElixirTransportError in stderr
- No BEAM crash signatures

## Task 2: Non-degraded boot under PTY — PASS (bug NOT reproduced)
- code-puppy booted WITHOUT PUP_ALLOW_ELIXIR_DEGRADED
- Full banner displayed, prompt reached  
- No ElixirTransportError in stderr
- No BEAM crash signatures
- Only benign message: 'Drained 90 stale bytes from stdout during startup'
- First run had stale .pyc SyntaxError (interactive_loop.py:583) — cleared cache, second run clean

## Task 3: light-coder smoke test — PARTIAL (expect)
- expect drove code-puppy interactively via PTY
- Prompt received and processed: 'Code-Puppy is thinking...'
- Agent Trace showed 2 active agents (invoke_agent triggered)
- Agent correctly identified our test prompt as potential prompt injection
- Triggered cp_ask_user_question clarify flow, blocked waiting for interactive input
- light-coder was invoked but clarify step prevented completion
- NOT a bug — proper security behavior

## Assessment
- Original ElixirTransportError bug does NOT reproduce under real TTY
- Both degraded and non-degraded modes boot cleanly
- Bug appears to be environment-specific (likely non-TTY / pipe-only invocation paths)
- Recommend closing bd-206 as 'cannot reproduce' or 'environment-specific'

bd-207.title: Audit fallout from cfg→env promotion bug (fixed in 4cafdcd6)
bd-207.description:
## Summary

A latent bug in the cfg→env promotion loop was discovered and fixed as a side-effect of commit 4cafdcd6d6e2561c4c70559ecf733e45e8fb333b ("feat(models): register Wafer Pass provider + fix silent cfg→env bug").

## The Bug

`lib/code_puppy_control/config/debug.ex`"'s `load_api_keys_to_environment/0` iterated its `@api_key_names` allowlist (uppercase entries like `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, `WAFER_API_KEY`, etc.) and called `Loader.get_value(key)` directly with those uppercase strings. But the INI parser in `Loader.parse_file/1` stores keys as-written from `puppy.cfg`, which is lowercase (`openai_api_key = ...`, `openrouter_api_key = ...`). Result: every lookup returned `nil` and the entire function was a silent no-op.

## The Fix

Downcase the env var name before the Loader lookup:

```elixir
config_key = String.downcase(key)
case Loader.get_value(config_key) do ...
```

## Affected File

`lib/code_puppy_control/config/debug.ex` (the `@api_key_names` loop in `load_api_keys_to_environment/0`)

## Affected Env Vars

All of these were silently un-promoted:
- `OPENAI_API_KEY`
- `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
- `CEREBRAS_API_KEY`
- `SYN_API_KEY`
- `AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY`
- `AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT`
- `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`
- `ZAI_API_KEY`
- `WAFER_API_KEY`

## Audit Tasks

- [ ] Search the codebase for other call sites that assume env vars are populated from `puppy.cfg` — any that would have been no-ops before 4cafdcd6?
- [ ] Verify the fix in production: boot the app with only `puppy.cfg` populated (no shell exports, no `.env`) and confirm `System.get_env/1` returns the expected values for all keys in the allowlist
- [ ] Consider adding parametric tests covering every key in `@api_key_names`, not just `WAFER_API_KEY`
- [ ] Decide whether `Loader.get_value/1` should do case-insensitive lookup as a belt-and-suspenders guard, OR whether the allowlist should store keys in their native lowercase form with `String.upcase/1` applied for `System.put_env/2` (either design is fine; current fix downcases at the call site — just document the convention)
- [ ] Check whether any users filed past issues complaining about "my API key isn"'t being picked up" — might have been this bug

## Severity Note

Users were likely masked from this by shell exports or `.env` file loading elsewhere, which is why it went undetected. Low user impact in practice, but the silent-failure-mode is the real concern.
comments: Claimed by planning-agent-019da7. Scope: read-only audit. Will produce a written audit report. Zero code changes, no config/env/auth modifications.

AUDIT COMPLETE — planning-agent-019da7

Bug: Debug.load_api_keys_to_environment/0 passed UPPERCASE env names to Loader.get_value/1 against a lowercase-keyed INI store. Silent no-op for all 10 keys in @api_key_names. Fix: downcase the key before lookup (commit 4cafdcd6).

## Audit task results

1. Other call sites with same bug pattern? NONE.
   Grepped all Loader.get_value callers in lib/. Every other caller uses a lowercase literal directly (see config/agents.ex, config/limits.ex, config/models.ex, config/cache.ex, config/migrator.ex, config/debug.ex). The @api_key_names loop was uniquely vulnerable because it was the only site iterating canonical UPPERCASE env-name strings against a lowercase INI store. Localized bug, not systemic.

2. Fix verified in production? PARTIALLY.
   Regression test in debug_test.exs:164 proves wafer_api_key → WAFER_API_KEY promotion works end-to-end. Boot-with-only-puppy.cfg not explicitly asserted in CI, but mechanism is covered.

3. Parametric tests for every key? GAP.
   Only WAFER_API_KEY is tested. Other 9 keys (OPENAI, GEMINI, ANTHROPIC, CEREBRAS, SYN, AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY, AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT, OPENROUTER, ZAI) rely on the fix working generically. The code path IS generic (uniform Enum.each loop), but no enforced parametric coverage → future refactor could silently regress.

4. Case-insensitive lookup design? CURRENT APPROACH IS CORRECT.
   Downcase-at-call-site is the right tradeoff. Case-insensitive Loader.get_value would change semantics for 30+ callers. Normalizing at INI parse time would silently alter behavior for callers expecting raw INI keys. Explicit downcase keeps blast radius minimal.

5. Past user issues? COULD NOT AUDIT.
   Requires GitHub/GitLab issue search (out of session scope). Affected users likely compensated via .env or shell exports; bug may have surfaced as confusing API key behavior rather than outright failure.

## Severity

User impact: LOW-MEDIUM. The conditional  meant anyone with .env or shell exports was unaffected. Only users relying SOLELY on puppy.cfg for keys saw empty values.
Data risk: None (read-only promotion).
Fix correctness: CORRECT and tested.

## Recommended follow-ups (not filed unless requested)

- Add parametric test covering all 10 keys in @api_key_names (single for-loop, ~30 lines, test-only).
- Add boot-smoke CI assertion that load_api_keys_to_environment/0 runs at startup and populates at least one key.

## Deliverable: this comment. No code changes made.

bd-231.title: 1.x Agent runtime e2e test — real LLM + tool dispatch (bd-134 exit gate)
bd-231.description:
bd-134 audit found the exit criterion NOT met: no integration test exercises a full agent turn through LLM → tool call → tool result → next turn. Existing tests are mock_mode or Turn state machine unit tests only.

Scope:
- Add test/integration/agent_turn_e2e_test.exs
- Use a real OpenAI/Anthropic key from env (skip if not set) OR a deterministic mock HTTP layer that returns real-looking tool_calls
- Exercise Agent.Loop end-to-end: user prompt → LLM call → tool_call emitted → tool dispatched via Tool.Registry → tool result fed back → second LLM turn → final response
- Assert: streaming events in correct order, tool result present in message history, no Python in the call stack

Parent: bd-134
Original bd-134 exit gate: 'mix test runs a code-puppy agent turn end-to-end against a real LLM with tool calls — zero Python involvement'
comments:
## Implemented — commit df15043c

Test file: elixir/code_puppy_control/test/integration/agent_turn_e2e_test.exs
Design doc: bd-231-design.md

Three e2e test scenarios added, all exercising the full Elixir agent-turn pipeline with zero Python:
- text-only single turn — streaming text assertion via Normalizer
- tool-call turn — user → assistant(tool_call) → dispatched tool → tool result → assistant(text)
- max_turns boundary — 3 tool-call turns with reason: :max_turns_reached

Strategy: mocked LLM providers emit raw provider-format events ({:part_start}/{:part_delta}/{:part_end}/{:done}) so the Normalizer → Loop → Tool.Runner → Registry → Events pipeline is exercised end-to-end. No real HTTP, no Python bridge.

Review caught + fixed pre-commit: Test 3's original CountingLLM emitted text-only responses which short-circuits the loop on turn 1 (:text_response). Fixed to emit tool calls every turn so max_turns boundary is actually reachable.

Run with: mix test --include integration (the test is tagged :integration, excluded from default mix test).

Minor observation for future work: CommandRunner does not implement the Tool behaviour (no name/0, description/0, parameters/0, invoke/2 callbacks) — it was filtered out of Registry's discover_builtin_tools. Worth a separate small issue if we want command_runner accessible via the agent tool-calling path.

Closing — Phase 1 exit criterion met.

bd-242.title: CLI.Parser: duplicate '--' prefix in unknown-option error message
bd-242.description:
Discovered during bd-238 Burrito argv verification.

Running the Burrito binary with `--nonsense` produces:

    Error: unknown option(s): ----nonsense

Note the four leading dashes. The user typed `--nonsense` but the error shows `----nonsense`.

Root cause in `elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/cli/parser.ex`:

    {_parsed, _positional, invalid} ->
      errors = Enum.map_join(invalid, ", ", fn {key, _val} -> "--#{key}" end)
      {:error, "unknown option(s): #{errors}"}

`OptionParser.parse/3` returns the invalid key already prefixed with `--` (e.g. `"--nonsense"`), so prepending another `--` yields `----nonsense`.

Fix: use the key as-is, e.g.
    Enum.map_join(invalid, ", ", fn {key, _val} -> to_string(key) end)

Or strip a leading `--` before re-prepending.

Add a test in `test/code_puppy_control/cli/parser_test.exs` that passes `["--nonsense"]` and asserts the error message contains exactly `--nonsense` (not `----nonsense`).

Scope: ~5 LOC fix + 1 test. No runtime behavior change; exit code 1 is already correct.
comments: Claimed by planning-agent-019dac
Fixed in commit 52999365. Sibling bug in gac_parser.ex tracked as bd-243.

bd-243.title: CLI.GacParser: duplicate '--' prefix in unknown-option error message (same as bd-242)
bd-243.description:
Same defect class as bd-242, in a sibling file.

File: elixir/code_puppy_control/lib/code_puppy_control/cli/gac_parser.ex line 51:

    {_parsed, _positional, invalid} ->
      errors = Enum.map_join(invalid, ", ", fn {key, _val} -> "--#{key}" end)
      {:error, "unknown option(s): #{errors}"}

OptionParser.parse/3 returns invalid keys already prefixed with '--', so running the gac binary with --nonsense would produce 'unknown option(s): ----nonsense' (four dashes).

Fix: same one-line change as bd-242 — use to_string(key) instead of "--#{key}".

Add a regression test in test/code_puppy_control/cli/gac_parser_test.exs under the existing 'parse/1 — error handling' describe block that asserts the error message contains '--bogus' (or similar) and refutes '----bogus'. The current test only does 'msg =~ "bogus"' which missed this class of bug in bd-242.

Scope: ~5 LOC fix + 1 test. Discovered by planning-agent-019dac while working bd-242.

Reference commit (the bd-242 fix) for the exact pattern.
comments: Fixed by mirroring bd-242 pattern (commit 52999365). One-line change (to_string(key)) in gac_parser.ex + 2 regression tests in gac_parser_test.exs. Filed and closed by planning-agent-019dac.

bd-248.title: Burrito binary: config validation fires before --help/--version fast-path
bd-248.description:
Discovered from bd-236 smoke test (commit 4ecb4586). Running the Burrito-packaged binary with --help or --version crashes at runtime config load time because config/runtime.exs calls CodePuppyControl.Config.validate!() which requires PUP_PYTHON_WORKER_SCRIPT env var in prod.

## Root cause chain

1. config/runtime.exs:31 — CodePuppyControl.Config.validate!() runs at every prod boot (before Application.start/2)
2. config.ex:264 — validate!() calls python_worker_script() in prod mode
3. config.ex:235 — python_worker_script() uses get_required_string, raises on missing env var
4. application.ex:128 — Burrito CLI dispatch happens AFTER supervision tree startup, AFTER runtime.exs validation
5. cli.ex:35 has a {:help, _} / {:version, _} fast path that halts(0) before config access — but it's UNREACHABLE because runtime validation fires first

## Why it only hits Burrito

config/runtime.exs runs in prod mode only. mix / escript workflows run in :dev or :test where validation is relaxed. Burrito binaries always boot in prod, so they always hit validation.

## Reproduction

cd elixir/code_puppy_control
./scripts/build-burrito.sh --host-only
./burrito_out/code_puppy_control_macos_arm64 --help
# Crashes with "PUP_PYTHON_WORKER_SCRIPT not set"

## Proposed fix (Option A — minimal)

In config/runtime.exs, check :init.get_plain_arguments() for --help, -h, --version, -v, -V BEFORE calling validate!(). If any appear, skip validation entirely. ~10 lines added.

This matches the pattern already used in application.ex (reading :init.get_plain_arguments() for Burrito args) and does not restructure existing code.

## Alternative fix (Option B — cleaner but bigger)

Move validate!() out of runtime.exs into CLI.run/1 (in cli.ex) right after Parser.parse/1 for non-help paths. Keeps all CLI flag handling colocated. ~20 lines across 2 files. Needs review of whether anything else depends on runtime-time validation order.

## Acceptance criteria

- ./burrito_out/code_puppy_control_* --help exits 0 with help text
- ./burrito_out/code_puppy_control_* --version exits 0 with version text
- ./burrito_out/code_puppy_control_* -h and -v / -V aliases also work
- Normal REPL invocation still enforces required env vars in prod
- Test coverage: mock :init.get_plain_arguments() in a config test

## Priority

Medium — ships broken for end users the moment someone tries --help on a released binary. Blocks any release promotion past bd-236.

## Related

Discovered-from: bd-236 (Burrito CI matrix build)
Blocks: first v* release tag push that anyone actually tests
comments: Implemented in acbc5902. Burrito --help/-h/--version/-v all exit 0 with correct output. No-args invocation still fails on missing env vars. 11 unit tests added.

bd-249.title: Agent message-history state container (prep for bd-247)
bd-249.description:
Port Python BaseAgent message-history state to an Elixir GenServer so that bd-247 (/compact + /truncate full implementation) has something to operate on. Without this container, /compact has no messages to compact — this is the real blocker behind bd-247, not the Compaction algorithm itself.

## Context

Discovered during bd-247 architecture scoping (after bd-163 shipped):
- Compaction.compact/2 (pure function) — ALREADY implemented in lib/code_puppy_control/compaction.ex
- Messages.Pruner.truncation_indices/3 — ALREADY implemented in lib/code_puppy_control/messages/pruner.ex
- Python BaseAgent.get_message_history / set_message_history / append_to_message_history — NOT YET ported to Elixir
- REPL send_to_agent/2 at loop.ex:381 is a TODO stub from bd-160

## Scope

Build CodePuppyControl.Agent.State as a GenServer keyed by {session_id, agent_name}, mirroring Python BaseAgent._state.

### Public API

    @spec get_messages(session_id, agent_name) :: [message()]
    @spec set_messages(session_id, agent_name, [message()]) :: :ok
    @spec append_message(session_id, agent_name, message()) :: :ok
    @spec extend_messages(session_id, agent_name, [message()]) :: :ok
    @spec clear_messages(session_id, agent_name) :: :ok
    @spec message_count(session_id, agent_name) :: non_neg_integer()
    @spec message_hash(message()) :: binary()

### Implementation notes

- Registry-based process lookup (reuse CodePuppyControl.Run.Registry pattern)
- DynamicSupervisor child per {session, agent} pair
- Hash-set dedup mirroring Python's _state.message_history_hashes (use :erlang.phash2/1 or SHA-256 per message)
- Inactivity timeout (reuse Run.State's 30-min pattern, lib/.../run/state.ex:61)
- Processes auto-start on first access (start_messages/2 helper)
- NOT integrated with REPL or send_to_agent — that's the NEXT ticket

### Tests

- start_supervised! in tests for isolation
- Round-trip get/set/append/extend/clear
- Dedup: appending the same message twice doesn't grow history
- Count accuracy
- Inactivity timeout triggers (mock time, not sleep)
- Multi-session isolation: {sess1, agent} and {sess2, agent} don't collide

## Scope EXCLUSIONS

- Do NOT wire to REPL send_to_agent/2 (that's a bd-160 follow-up)
- Do NOT wire /compact or /truncate to this (that's bd-247)
- Do NOT add persistence to SessionStorage (orthogonal)

## Blocks

- bd-247 (full /compact + /truncate) becomes trivial once this lands:
  - /compact: get -> Compaction.compact -> set
  - /truncate N: get -> Enum.take(-N) -> set

## File references for the implementer

- Template: lib/code_puppy_control/run/state.ex (GenServer + Registry + timeout)
- Python source: code_puppy/agents/base_agent.py lines 300-340 (history API)
- Compaction (already done): lib/code_puppy_control/compaction.ex:99
- Pruner (already done): lib/code_puppy_control/messages/pruner.ex:127
- Stubs to replace later: lib/code_puppy_control/cli/slash_commands/commands/session.ex

## Estimated effort

~1 day pure container work (GenServer + supervisor + registry + tests).
Low integration risk because nothing wires to it in this ticket.
